input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle physical twin featuring teleoperated base hardware retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Main hardware components include lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements. Operational domain is Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) within Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), characterized by resource-constrained ECUs requiring computation offloading, real-time data monitoring, and remote management capabilities for safety-critical operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three key services: 1) Data Monitoring via a user interface (UI) for real-time visualization of vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks, along with data saving and report generation; 2) Computation Offloading of the ACC system (including lead vehicle detection, speed calculation based on distance, and actuation command generation) to cloud-based or higher-capacity hardware; 3) Remote Management enabling operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, and issue emergency braking commands remotely. Target users are operators for system monitoring and management, and developers for troubleshooting using data collection and monitoring capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Clock synchronization requires 100 ms accuracy for computation offloading in safety-critical vehicular applications, constrained by hardware limitations despite industry standards typically mandating stricter synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Three distinct entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) deployed on separate hardware components, with the User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel subcomponents; Physical Twin Entity handles resource-specific functions including Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: ISO 23247 RA-based architecture implemented using ROS2 packages where each FE is a ROS2 node, organized into three entities deployed on distinct hardware; Physical Twin Entity departs from standard by hosting general automotive functionality such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw LiDAR point cloud data, vehicle state information (e.g., speed, steering angle), and object tracks are transmitted from physical sensors to the Data Collecting FE. The Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component processes raw point clouds into structured tracks. Data Translation serializes data, aligns message types, and converts units. Intra-component communication uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi using UDP. Data quality is maintained through clustering, tracking, and preprocessing steps to filter noise and ensure accurate representation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), steering, motor, and servo actuation are processed by the ACC Controlling FE and transmitted via ROS2 network to the Actuation FE. The ISO 23247 Data Translation component handles serialization and message alignment for control signals.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Physical Twin Entity is deployed on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer. Each entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entity) is hosted on separate hardware components as per Section IV.B. Data is stored on a device external to the vehicle as specified in Section III.A (R3), with edge computing for real-time processing and cloud storage for persistent data.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247 standard, specifically Part 2 (Reference Architecture), which structures the Digital Twin into Observable Manufacturing Elements (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Part 4 (Information exchange) governs data protocols, with OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) implemented for interoperable data collection and device control.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical twin as a '1/10-scale autonomous vehicle' built from a 'teleoperated base hardware retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology', which matches the source: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th]-scale autonomous vehicle... built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle... by retrofitting it with autonomy components.' It correctly identifies the main hardware components as 'lower and upper chassis layers and autonomy elements', supported by the caption in Fig. 1: 'Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level chassis, and the autonomy elements.' The operational domain is correctly identified as ACC within ADAS, and the characteristic 'resource-constrained ECUs requiring computation offloading' is supported by the text: 'DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient.' The extraction is highly specific and well-grounded in the sources.

The extraction correctly lists and details the three specific services provided by the Digital Twin. For Data Monitoring, it matches the source: 'The DT presented in this case will contain a user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports of vehicle state data... the UI will display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks.' For Computation Offloading, it accurately describes offloading the ACC system logic (lead vehicle detection, speed calculation) to the DT/cloud, citing: 'we demonstrate this functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance to the lead vehicle...'. For Remote Management, it correctly lists enabling/disabling ACC, setting speed, and emergency braking, supported by: 'the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.' The target users (operators and developers) are also correctly identified based on the text: 'The users of our system can be grouped into two classes: operators and developers.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly mention several tools and technologies used to implement the Digital Twin, such as 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)', 'Python 3.8', 'C++', 'MATLAB/Simulink', and 'ISO 23247' as the reference architecture. Therefore, the claim is incorrect, and the score is 1.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and directly supported by the DTS requirements section, which details the synchronization accuracy and the reasoning behind it. The evidence confirms the 100 ms requirement for computation offloading and the constraint of hardware limitations compared to general standards. Quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.' and 'Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The extraction accurately captures the system structure, including the separation of entities onto different hardware, the splitting of the User Interface FE, and the specific functionality of the Physical Twin Entity. This is strongly grounded in the architecture description. Quotes: 'User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components.' and 'Physical Twin Entity... can host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.' and 'considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component'

The extraction correctly identifies the use of the ISO 23247 RA, the implementation method (ROS2 packages/nodes), the deployment strategy, and the specific architectural deviation regarding the Physical Twin Entity. The text provides detailed support for these claims. Quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA... implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package.' and 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. After reviewing the source text, which focuses on the internal architecture of the Digital Twin System (DTS), its internal communication (ROS2, Wi-Fi), and the ISO 23247 reference architecture, there is no information regarding integration with external systems, enterprise-level interoperability standards, or dependencies on systems outside the specific DTS implementation described. Therefore, the claim that it is not in the document is correct.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source provides detailed information about the computational models and data components. Specifically, it describes the ACC model, point cloud clustering and tracking (using DBSCAN and Kalman filters), and data storage in a SQL database. Quotes: 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter', 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed', 'Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source identifies specific physical actuators and control mechanisms. The text lists the electronic speed controller, motor, steering servo, and the actuation software component. Quotes: 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'lower level chassis is responsible for controlling the steering and the speed of the vehicle'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source explicitly describes the sensing infrastructure on the physical twin. It lists specific sensor models and types. Quotes: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'Streams of data from LiDAR, cameras, and other sensors are fused in real time'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the text does not contain the specific details required by the characteristic description. While the text mentions general 'high-fidelity' definitions and constraints like '1/10th-scale' and latency requirements ('maintain network latency of at most 100 ms'), it lacks descriptions of 'validation and verification methods', 'uncertainty sources and quantification', or 'error detection and correction mechanisms' for the models.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the data flow, components (Point Cloud Clustering, Data Translation), and protocols (ROS2, Wi-Fi) described in the text. It correctly identifies the functions of the Data Translation FE ('serializes data, aligns message types'). However, it mentions 'UDP' which is cut off in the source text ('using U'), and attributes 'filter noise' to the clustering process, which is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the text.

The extraction correctly describes the flow from the ACC Controlling FE to the Actuation FE via ROS2 and mentions the Data Translation component's role. It accurately reflects the control commands for ACC and actuation. However, it implies 'steering' is processed by the ACC Controlling FE, whereas the text specifically links ACC to speed control ('desired speed') while steering is a general function of the vehicle/Actuation FE.

The extraction is a near-verbatim summary of the source text regarding the network infrastructure. It correctly identifies the intra-component communication (ROS2) and inter-entity communication (Wi-Fi). While the source text snippet ends abruptly with 'using U' (likely truncating 'UDP'), the extraction's description is highly specific and strongly grounded in the provided evidence for the visible portions.

The extraction correctly details the hardware deployment (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano) and the separation of entities onto different hardware components. It accurately reflects the requirement for external data storage (R3). However, it introduces unsupported claims about 'cloud storage' and 'edge computing' which are not found in the provided snippets; the text only mentions 'a device external to the vehicle' and a 'SQL database'.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The source text does not mention lifecycle phases, development stages, transition management, or lifecycle governance approaches for the Digital Twin. It focuses on the architecture and implementation of a specific case study.

The source text does not describe engineering methodologies, quality assurance, testing, validation, version control, or evolution management strategies. It details the architectural design and service implementation but not the broader twinning process.

While the text mentions 'Data Monitoring' and 'Remote Management' services, it does not elaborate on types of insights, analytics capabilities, decision-making processes, user interaction methods, or confidence measures for recommendations. The focus is on architecture and services, not insights generation.

The extraction is highly specific and accurately reflects the detailed description of the ISO 23247 standard provided in Section II.A.
